title: The Happy Home Initiative
sub: Revolutionizing real estate through transparency — a buyer-facing platform that surfaces the data agents won't.

tldr.summary: A buyer-facing real estate platform designed to surface information agents typically gate. The core challenge: how do you build trust in a domain defined by information asymmetry?

challenge.label: 01 · THE CHALLENGE
challenge.heading:
The question nobody
was asking
challenge.lede: Every buyer in the room was wondering the same thing: "what's this really worth?"
challenge.body:
Real estate platforms optimized for agent acquisition — more leads, faster closings — systematically buried the data buyers needed to make confident decisions. The question "How do I know this isn't marked up?" appeared in 7 of 9 research interviews.

The design challenge was precise: give buyers the same view the agent has.
challenge.researchFindings: Three pain-points dominated the affinity map: opacity (hidden pricing history), overwhelm (too many unranked filters), and urgency-without-context ("3 people viewing now" with no market anchor).

steps.title: Stakeholder alignment
steps.desc: Two workshops to align on success metrics before wireframes touched Figma.

steps.title: Contextual inquiry
steps.desc: Shadowed 4 buyers during active searches — mapped every moment of doubt.

steps.title: Prototype + test (R1)
steps.desc: 6 usability sessions, 14 iterations — focused on information hierarchy.

steps.title: Accessibility pass
steps.desc: WCAG AA across all states; contrast ratios documented in the DS.

steps.title: Handoff + measure
steps.desc: Developer pairing over 3 sprints; analytics instrumented at key funnels.

iterations.desc: Narrative-first layout — felt too editorial, buried the data.
Added pricing history inline — improved trust scores +31%.
Final: data first, story as context — highest task completion.

impact.label: 03 · THE IMPACT
impact.heading:
The app made it
so easy and fun
impact.quote: "For the first time I felt like I was on equal footing with my agent."
takeaways: Defer attachment. Users who saw pricing history first were less likely to emotionally anchor on asking price.
Show the data, then frame it. Raw numbers without context increase anxiety — the transparency layer worked because it was curated, not dumped.
Anticipate the second move. The best UX decision was the 'Similar sold' panel — users didn't ask for it, but 68% used it.
nextMoves: Extend transparency model to rental market.
A/B test AI-assisted price prediction overlay.
Partner API for public records integration.

title: Idram
sub: Mobile banking for Armenia's largest fintech — a calmer, denser feed for power users.

hero.title:
Redesigning Armenia's
largest mobile
banking app.

tldr.summary: Idram is Armenia's most-used digital payment platform. The redesign focused on reducing cognitive load for power users managing multiple accounts and frequent transactions.

challenge.label: 01 · THE CHALLENGE
challenge.heading:
Too much data,
not enough clarity
challenge.lede: Power users were drowning in transaction noise.
challenge.body:
Idram's original dashboard showed all transaction types with equal visual weight — a receipts flood that made finding the 'important' nearly impossible.

The core question: how do you make a dense information environment feel calm?
challenge.researchFindings: Key findings: 73% of users checked the app daily but found navigation 'overwhelming'. Top 3 actions (transfer, top-up, bill pay) were buried 3+ taps deep.

steps.title: Usage analytics audit
steps.desc: Mapped 90-day user flows to surface the true top-5 jobs.

steps.title: Card sorting
steps.desc: 32 participants grouped 40 features — revealed a 3-cluster mental model.

steps.title: Hierarchy redesign
steps.desc: Moved top actions to bottom nav; secondary features in contextual drawers.

steps.title: Visual density work
steps.desc: Reduced transaction list line-height 22→16px — fits 6 vs 4 items without crowding.

steps.title: Prototype testing
steps.desc: 8 sessions, measured task completion time — 40% faster on key flows.

steps.title: Accessibility audit
steps.desc: Full WCAG AA compliance; high-contrast mode added for outdoor use.

iterations.desc: All transactions equal weight — mirrored the original problem.
Date + category grouping — reduced scanning time by 28%.
Smart grouping + balance prominance — clearest hierarchy.

impact.label: 03 · THE IMPACT
impact.heading:
Banking that feels
calm and fast
impact.quote: "It used to feel like work. Now it actually feels like a banking app."
takeaways: Hierarchy beats hierarchy. Visual density ≠ cognitive load — the redesign is technically denser but feels lighter because priority is clear.
Analytics lie about frequency, not about pain. The most complained-about flow (transfers) wasn't the most-used — it was just the most broken.
Bottom nav for finance is non-negotiable. One-handed operation for an app people use on commutes is a usability multiplier.
nextMoves: Personalized dashboard ordering based on usage patterns.
Biometric auth improvements for faster access.
Widget support for iOS/Android home screens.

title: Auto — Roadside
sub: On-demand mechanics for stranded drivers, freelance pros, and fleet managers.

hero.title:
Help dispatched to
your location in
6 minutes.
hero.bannerLabel: Driver app · Mechanic app · Fleet dashboard

tldr.summary: Auto serves three user types — stranded drivers, independent mechanics, and fleet managers — from a single design system. The challenge: one platform, three radically different mental models.

challenge.label: 01 · THE CHALLENGE
challenge.heading:
Three users,
one platform
challenge.lede: Locked out? Flat tire? Dead battery? We'll dispatch help immediately.
challenge.body:
Auto needed to serve three audiences with fundamentally different goals: drivers want speed and certainty; mechanics want efficiency and earnings; fleet managers want oversight and analytics.

The design challenge: build one system that feels native to each.
challenge.researchFindings: Drivers prioritize ETA and trust — they need to feel safe. Mechanics prioritize job quality and routing efficiency. Fleet managers need aggregate views and exception alerts.

steps.title: Parallel JTBD mapping
steps.desc: Ran 3 simultaneous journey maps — found 6 shared interaction patterns.

steps.title: Design system first
steps.desc: Built the DS before any screen — every component maps to a user need.

steps.title: Driver app (MVP)
steps.desc: Emergency-first flow: location → dispatch → ETA → mechanic profile.

steps.title: Mechanic app
steps.desc: Earnings-first dashboard: today's jobs, routing, and income tracking.

steps.title: Fleet back-office
steps.desc: Web dashboard: vehicle status, staff scheduling, analytics.

steps.title: Cross-app testing
steps.desc: Ran end-to-end sessions with all 3 user types simultaneously.

iterations.desc: Single app with role switching — users confused by context changes.
Three apps, shared DS — each app focused, brand consistent.
Separated apps + shared onboarding flow — best of both.

impact.label: 03 · THE IMPACT
impact.heading:
Help arrives in
6 minutes flat
impact.quote: "When my tire blew at midnight, the app was the calmest thing in that moment."
takeaways: Emergency UX requires radical simplicity. In high-stress moments, users don't read — they tap. Every emergency flow was designed for one-thumb operation.
Earnings visibility changes behavior. Mechanics who could see their projected daily earnings took 23% more jobs than those who couldn't.
Shared design systems compound. Building the DS upfront cost 2 extra weeks but saved 6 weeks across three parallel builds.
nextMoves: Predictive dispatch using traffic and mechanic location data.
In-app payment and invoicing for mechanics.
Integration with insurance APIs for covered roadside.
